metadata.title: The Math of Being Afraid Together: Co-op Horror Design
metadata.color_semantics: claude fidelity: INK #3D3929; SPARK #D97757 one accent.
metadata.author: Seth Brown
tags: The Math of Being Afraid Together: Co-op
metadata.note: HAI Fellows batch v2 — Teardown register, @NikBearBrown, Remotion animated bookends. Credit: Seth Brown — Humanitarians AI Fellow. HONESTY: all numbers trace to source article.

beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. Phasmophobia has sold over 23 million copies, so its fear-generating design is worth taking seriously as a repeatable mechanism. Co-op horror produces fear because players cannot dominate the environment, keeping them vulnerable rather than in control. The strongest test of a claim is a case that strips away everything incidental to see what remains. Haunt & Harvest is a tabletop game with no rendering, audio engine, or monster AI, yet the fear mechanism still holds.

artifactLines: Phasmophobia has sold over 23 million copies, so its fear-generating design is worth taking seriously as a repeatable mechanism.
Co-op horror produces fear because players cannot dominate the environment, keeping them vulnerable rather than in control.
The strongest test of a claim is a case that strips away everything incidental to see what remains.
Haunt & Harvest is a tabletop game with no rendering, audio engine, or monster AI, yet the fear mechanism still holds.

beats.narration_text: The claim that co-op horror works because players cannot dominate the environment fascinates me, especially since Haunt & Harvest strips away rendering, audio, and monster AI yet still generates fear. I want to test where the mechanism actually lives. Can you (1) list the specific tabletop rules in Haunt & Harvest that enforce shared vulnerability, (2) map each one to its Phasmophobia digital equivalent, and (3) design a minimal card-only prototype that preserves the fear and predict which rule, if removed, collapses it first?

output: Start with a fact that should not be true
This piece is a proof, not a tour

beats.narration_text: It did not stop working. Phasmophobia has sold over 23 million copies.
